perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue in full force and effect for an initial period of five (5) years. Thereafter, this Agreement shall automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods unless either party provides the other party with written notification at least thirty (30) days prior to the expiration of the initial five (5) year term or any one (1) year renewal thereof of its intention to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue in full force and effect for an initial period of five (5) years. Thereafter, this Agreement shall automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods unless either party provides the other party with written notification at least ten (10) days prior to the expiration of the initial five (5) year term or any one (1) year renewal thereof of its intention to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for termination from 30 days to 10 days may violate California law regarding contract termination notice periods, especially if the contract involves significant financial obligations or unique services where a longer notice period is more reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1657 states that if no time is specified for an act to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. Ten days might be deemed unreasonable for a complex agreement requiring substantial wind-down, potentially contradicting this statute.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification period for contract termination from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that California Civil Code § 1657 requires a 'reasonable time' if no time is specified. While the snippet does not explicitly define what constitutes a reasonable time, the Perturbation Explanation suggests this timeframe might be insufficient for complex agreements requiring substantial wind-down. Because the perturbation falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, this change creates a legally questionable situation, making the contract potentially unenforceable, therefore, a contradiction exists. The original text established 30 days and the changed text reduces it to 10, which could be deemed unreasonable.

perturbation.original_text: Such books and records shall be kept for at least three (3) years following the end of the calendar month to which they pertain, and shall be open for inspection by an independent certified public accountant reasonably acceptable to Corio for the purpose of verifying the amounts payable to Commerce One under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Such books and records shall be kept for at least one (1) year following the end of the calendar month to which they pertain, and shall be open for inspection by an independent certified public accountant reasonably acceptable to Corio for the purpose of verifying the amounts payable to Commerce One under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping duration to one year may violate California's statute of limitations for breach of contract claims, which is four years for written contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. By only requiring records to be kept for one year, the contract impedes the ability to pursue claims within the legally allowed period.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record-keeping duration from three years to one year. California Code of Civil Procedure § 337(a), as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon any contract, obligation, or liability founded upon a written instrument. By reducing the record-keeping duration to one year, the changed text directly impedes the ability to pursue claims within the legally allowed four-year period, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The arbitration shall be conducted by one (1) arbitrator appointed in accordance with the Rules in San Francisco County, California. A judgment upon the award may be entered in any court having jurisdiction of the parties, including without limitation the courts in San Francisco, California.
perturbation.changed_text: The arbitration shall be conducted by one (1) arbitrator appointed in accordance with the Rules in San Francisco County, California. The arbitrator's decision is final and not subject to judicial review, regardless of errors in fact or law.
perturbation.explanation: Preventing judicial review of arbitration decisions, regardless of errors, may violate California law, which allows for limited judicial review of arbitration awards, particularly for legal errors that substantially prejudice a party's rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1286.2 specifies grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including corruption, fraud, undue means, or substantial prejudice due to arbitrator misconduct or errors. Eliminating judicial review entirely contradicts this statute.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 4. Enforcement of the Award [1285 - 1288.8] ( Chapter 4 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) ARTICLE 1. Confirmation, Correction or Vacation of the Award [1285 - 1287.6] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1286.2. (a) Subject to Section 1286.4, the court shall vacate the award if the court determines any of the following: (1) The award was procured by corruption, fraud or other undue means. (2) There was corruption in any of the arbitrators. (3) The rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by misconduct of a neutral arbitrator. (4) The arbitrators exceeded their powers and the award cannot be corrected without affecting the merits of the decision upon the controversy submitted. (5) The rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by the refusal of the arbitrators to postpone the hearing upon sufficient cause being shown therefor or by the refusal of the arbitrators to hear evidence material to the controversy or by other conduct of the arbitrators contrary to the provisions of this title. (6) An arbitrator making the award eithe
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly eliminates judicial review of arbitration decisions, regardless of errors, which contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1286.2. This statute, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifies grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including instances where the rights of a party were substantially prejudiced by misconduct of a neutral arbitrator or if the arbitrators exceeded their powers. By precluding any judicial review, the modified text prevents a party from seeking recourse under these legally established grounds, thus creating a direct and serious legal contradiction. The change removes a core legal protection provided by California law.
